**Blairstown** is a [township](/wiki/Township_(New_Jersey) "Township (New Jersey)") in [Warren County](/wiki/Warren_County,_New_Jersey "Warren County, New Jersey"), in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"). As of the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the township's population was 5,704,[[8]](#cite_note-Census2020-8)[[9]](#cite_note-LWD2020-9) a decrease of 263 (−4.4%) from the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") count of 5,967,[[18]](#cite_note-Census2010-18)[[19]](#cite_note-LWD2010-19) which in turn reflected an increase of 220 (+3.8%) from the 5,747 counted in the [2000 census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

The area had been known as Smith's Mill and was later called Butts Bridge (variously spelled as "Butt's Bridge" and "Butts' Bridge"), named for a family that owned the eponymous crossing of the [Paulins Kill](/wiki/Paulins_Kill "Paulins Kill").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Blairstown was incorporated as a township by an act of the [New Jersey Legislature](/wiki/New_Jersey_Legislature "New Jersey Legislature") on April 14, 1845, from portions of [Knowlton Township](/wiki/Knowlton_Township,_New_Jersey "Knowlton Township, New Jersey"), based on the results of a referendum held that day.[[22]](#cite_note-Story-22) The township was named for [John Insley Blair](/wiki/John_Insley_Blair "John Insley Blair").[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Blairstown,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geography")]

According to the [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the township had a total area of 30.78 square miles (79.71 km2), including 29.89 square miles (77.41 km2) of land and 0.89 square miles (2.30 km2) of water (2.88%).[[1]](#cite_note-CensusArea-1)[[2]](#cite_note-GR1-2) The township is located in the [Kittatinny Valley](/wiki/Kittatinny_Mountains "Kittatinny Mountains") which is a section of the [Great Appalachian Valley](/wiki/Great_Appalachian_Valley "Great Appalachian Valley") that stretches for 700 miles (1,100 km) from Canada to Alabama.

[Blairstown CDP](/wiki/Blairstown_(CDP),_New_Jersey "Blairstown (CDP), New Jersey") (with a 2020 Census population of 493) is an [unincorporated community](/wiki/Local_government_in_New_Jersey#Unincorporated_communities "Local government in New Jersey") and [census-designated place](/wiki/Census-designated_place "Census-designated place") (CDP) located within the township.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-CPH232-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) A portion of [Hainesburg](/wiki/Hainesburg,_New_Jersey "Hainesburg, New Jersey") (with a 2020 Census population of 422), which is primarily located in [Knowlton Township](/wiki/Knowlton_Township,_New_Jersey "Knowlton Township, New Jersey"), falls within Blairstown's borders.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Other unincorporated communities, localities and place names located partially or completely within the township include Blair Lake, Cedar Lake, Cooks Pond, Jacksonburg, Kalarama, Lake Susquehanna, Mount Vernon, [Paulina](/wiki/Paulina,_New_Jersey "Paulina, New Jersey"), Vail and Walnut Valley.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

White Township borders the Warren County municipalities of [Frelinghuysen Township](/wiki/Frelinghuysen_Township,_New_Jersey "Frelinghuysen Township, New Jersey"), [Hardwick Township](/wiki/Hardwick_Township,_New_Jersey "Hardwick Township, New Jersey"), [Hope Township](/wiki/Hope_Township,_New_Jersey "Hope Township, New Jersey") and [Knowlton Township](/wiki/Knowlton_Township,_New_Jersey "Knowlton Township, New Jersey").[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### 2010 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Blairstown,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2010 census")]

The [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") counted 5,967 people, 2,124 households, and 1,703 families in the township. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 193.6 per square mile (74.7/km2). There were 2,272 housing units at an average density of 73.7 per square mile (28.5/km2). The racial makeup was 96.03% (5,730) [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._census) "White (U.S. census)"), 1.12% (67) [Black or African American](/wiki/Black_(U.S._census) "Black (U.S. census)"), 0.12% (7) [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._census) "Native American (U.S. census)"), 1.14% (68) [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._census) "Asian (U.S. census)"), 0.00% (0) [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_(U.S._census) "Pacific Islander (U.S. census)"), 0.45% (27) from [other races](/wiki/Race_and_ethnicity_in_the_United_States_census "Race and ethnicity in the United States census"), and 1.14% (68) from two or more races. [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._census) "Hispanic (U.S. census)") of any race were 3.79% (226) of the population.[[18]](#cite_note-Census2010-18)

Of the 2,124 households, 32.8% had children under the age of 18; 66.8% were married couples living together; 9.4% had a female householder with no husband present and 19.8% were non-families. Of all households, 15.8% were made up of individuals and 6.3% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.78 and the average family size was 3.11.[[18]](#cite_note-Census2010-18)

23.9% of the population were under the age of 18, 7.2% from 18 to 24, 20.1% from 25 to 44, 33.3% from 45 to 64, and 15.5% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 44.3 years. For every 100 females, the population had 99.4 males. For every 100 females ages 18 and older there were 96.8 males.[[18]](#cite_note-Census2010-18)

The Census Bureau's 2006–2010 [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") showed that (in 2010 [inflation-adjusted](/wiki/Inflation_adjustment "Inflation adjustment") dollars) [median household income](/wiki/Median_household_income "Median household income") was $82,952 (with a margin of error of +/− $10,269) and the median family income was $92,063 (+/− $14,594). Males had a median income of $73,818 (+/− $7,161) versus $54,959 (+/− $13,254) for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the borough was $38,393 (+/− $7,342). About 4.1% of families and 5.0% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 8.1% of those under age 18 and 1.4% of those age 65 or over.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

### 2000 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Blairstown,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2000 census")]

As of the [2000 United States census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census"),[[15]](#cite_note-GR2-15) there were 5,747 people, 2,040 households, and 1,638 families residing in the township. The population density was 185.3 inhabitants per square mile (71.5/km2). There were 2,136 housing units at an average density of 68.9 per square mile (26.6/km2). The racial makeup of the township was 98.17% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 0.26% [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)"), 0.14% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 0.56% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.02% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_(U.S._Census) "Pacific Islander (U.S. Census)"), 0.28% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 0.57% from two or more races. [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") of any race were 1.98% of the population.[[41]](#cite_note-Census2000-41)[[42]](#cite_note-Census2000SF1-42)

There were 2,040 households, out of which 35.0% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 69.6% were [married couples](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") living together, 7.3% had a female householder with no husband present, and 19.7% were non-families. 15.3% of all households were made up of individuals, and 7.1% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.81 and the average family size was 3.14.[[41]](#cite_note-Census2000-41)[[42]](#cite_note-Census2000SF1-42)

In the township the population was spread out, with 25.6% under the age of 18, 5.8% from 18 to 24, 26.7% from 25 to 44, 29.5% from 45 to 64, and 12.4% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 40 years. For every 100 females, there were 99.5 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 96.2 males.[[41]](#cite_note-Census2000-41)[[42]](#cite_note-Census2000SF1-42)

The median income for a household in the township was $64,809, and the median income for a family was $71,214. Males had a median income of $51,931 versus $33,646 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the township was $27,775. About 3.0% of families and 4.5% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 2.4% of those under age 18 and 3.4% of those age 65 or over.[[41]](#cite_note-Census2000-41)[[42]](#cite_note-Census2000SF1-42)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Blairstown,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Government")]

### Local government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Blairstown,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Local government")]

Blairstown is governed under the [Township](/wiki/Township_(New_Jersey) "Township (New Jersey)") form of New Jersey municipal government, one of 141 municipalities (of the 564) statewide that use this form, the second-most commonly used form of government in the state.[[45]](#cite_note-45) The Township Committee is composed of five members, who are elected directly by the voters [at-large](/wiki/At-large "At-large") in partisan elections to serve three-year terms of office on a staggered basis, with either one or two seats coming up for election each year as part of the November general election in a three-year cycle.[[6]](#cite_note-DataBook-6)[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47) At a reorganization meeting held each year during the first week of January, the Committee members select one of their members to serve as Mayor and another to serve as Deputy Mayor.[[3]](#cite_note-Government-3)

As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Blairstown,_New_Jersey&action=edit), the Blairstown Township Committee is comprised of [Mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor") Rob Moorhead ([R](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)"), term on committee ends December 31, 2027, term as mayor ends 2025), [Deputy Mayor](/wiki/Deputy_Mayor "Deputy Mayor") Walter Orcutt (R, term on committee ends 2026; term as deputy mayor ends 2025), Karen Lance (R, 2025), G. Eric Lohman (R, 2026) and Giovanna "JoAnne" VanValkenburg (R, 2027).[[3]](#cite_note-Government-3)[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-Warren2024-49)[[50]](#cite_note-Warren2023-50)[[51]](#cite_note-Warren2022-51)

### Federal, state, and county representation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Blairstown,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Federal, state, and county representation")]

Blairstown Township is located in the 7th Congressional District[[52]](#cite_note-PCR2022-52) and is part of New Jersey's 23rd state legislative district.[[53]](#cite_note-Districts2023-53)

For the [119th United States Congress](/wiki/119th_United_States_Congress "119th United States Congress"), [New Jersey's 7th congressional district](/wiki/New_Jersey%27s_7th_congressional_district "New Jersey's 7th congressional district") is represented by [Thomas Kean Jr.](/wiki/Thomas_Kean_Jr. "Thomas Kean Jr.") ([R](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)"), [Westfield](/wiki/Westfield,_New_Jersey "Westfield, New Jersey")).[[54]](#cite_note-54) New Jersey is represented in the [United States Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") by [Democrats](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") [Cory Booker](/wiki/Cory_Booker "Cory Booker") ([Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey"), term ends 2027) and [Andy Kim](/wiki/Andy_Kim "Andy Kim") ([Moorestown](/wiki/Moorestown,_New_Jersey "Moorestown, New Jersey"), term ends 2031).[[55]](#cite_note-55)

For the [2026–2027 session](/wiki/222nd_New_Jersey_Legislature "222nd New Jersey Legislature"), the [23rd legislative district](/wiki/New_Jersey%27s_23rd_legislative_district "New Jersey's 23rd legislative district") of the [New Jersey Legislature](/wiki/New_Jersey_Legislature "New Jersey Legislature") is represented in the [State Senate](/wiki/New_Jersey_Senate "New Jersey Senate") by [Doug Steinhardt](/wiki/Doug_Steinhardt "Doug Steinhardt") ([R](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)"), [Lopatcong Township](/wiki/Lopatcong_Township,_New_Jersey "Lopatcong Township, New Jersey")) and in the [General Assembly](/wiki/New_Jersey_General_Assembly "New Jersey General Assembly") by [John DiMaio](/wiki/John_DiMaio "John DiMaio") (R, [Hackettstown](/wiki/Hackettstown,_New_Jersey "Hackettstown, New Jersey")) and [Erik Peterson](/wiki/Erik_Peterson_(politician) "Erik Peterson (politician)") (R, [Franklin Township](/wiki/Franklin_Township,_Hunterdon_County,_New_Jersey "Franklin Township, Hunterdon County, New Jersey")).[[56]](#cite_note-56)

[Warren County](/wiki/Warren_County,_New_Jersey "Warren County, New Jersey") is governed by a three-member [Board of County Commissioners](/wiki/Board_of_County_Commissioners_(New_Jersey) "Board of County Commissioners (New Jersey)"), who are chosen [at-large](/wiki/At-large "At-large") on a staggered basis in partisan elections with one seat coming up for election each year as part of the November general election. At an annual reorganization meeting held in the beginning of January, the board selects one of its members to serve as Commissioner Director and other as Deputy Director.[[57]](#cite_note-57) As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Blairstown,_New_Jersey&action=edit), Warren County's Commissioners are:

Deputy Director Jason J. Sarnoski ([R](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)"), [Lopatcong Township](/wiki/Lopatcong_Township,_New_Jersey "Lopatcong Township, New Jersey"); 2025),[[58]](#cite_note-58)
Lori Ciesla (R, Lopatcong Township; 2026),[[59]](#cite_note-59) and
Director James R. Kern III (R, [Pohatcong Township](/wiki/Pohatcong_Township,_New_Jersey "Pohatcong Township, New Jersey"); 2027).[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)

Constitutional officers of Warren County are:
Clerk Holly Mackey (R, [Alpha](/wiki/Alpha,_New_Jersey "Alpha, New Jersey"); 2027),[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63)
Sheriff James McDonald Sr. (R, [Phillipsburg](/wiki/Phillipsburg,_New_Jersey "Phillipsburg, New Jersey"); 2025)[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65) and
Surrogate [Michael J. Doherty](/wiki/Michael_J._Doherty "Michael J. Doherty") (R, [Washington](/wiki/Washington_Township,_Warren_County,_New_Jersey "Washington Township, Warren County, New Jersey"); 2025).[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68)

### Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Blairstown,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Politics")]

As of March 2011, there were a total of 4,294 registered voters in Blairstown Township, of which 707 (16.5% vs. 21.5% countywide) were registered as [Democrats](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"), 1,882 (43.8% vs. 35.3%) were registered as [Republicans](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") and 1,702 (39.6% vs. 43.1%) were registered as [Unaffiliated](/wiki/Unaffiliated_(New_Jersey) "Unaffiliated (New Jersey)"). There were 3 voters registered as [Libertarians](/wiki/Libertarian_Party_(United_States) "Libertarian Party (United States)") or [Greens](/wiki/Green_Party_(United_States) "Green Party (United States)").[[69]](#cite_note-VoterRegistration-69) Among the township's 2010 Census population, 72.0% (vs. 62.3% in Warren County) were registered to vote, including 94.6% of those ages 18 and over (vs. 81.5% countywide).[[69]](#cite_note-VoterRegistration-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Blairstown,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Education")]

Further information: [Blairstown Township School District](/wiki/Blairstown_Township_School_District "Blairstown Township School District")

The [Blairstown Township School District](/wiki/Blairstown_Township_School_District "Blairstown Township School District") serves public school students in [kindergarten](/wiki/Kindergarten "Kindergarten") through [sixth grade](/wiki/Sixth_grade "Sixth grade") at the Blairstown Elementary School.[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-WarrenDistricts-80) Students from [Hardwick Township](/wiki/Hardwick_Township,_New_Jersey "Hardwick Township, New Jersey"), a non-operating school district attend Blairstown Elementary School.[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82) As of the 2021–22 school year, the district, comprised of one school, had an enrollment of 435 students and 42.0 classroom teachers (on an [FTE](/wiki/Full-time_equivalent "Full-time equivalent") basis), for a [student–teacher ratio](/wiki/Student%E2%80%93teacher_ratio "Student–teacher ratio") of 10.4:1.[[83]](#cite_note-NCES-83)

Students in [seventh](/wiki/Seventh_grade "Seventh grade") through [twelfth grades](/wiki/Twelfth_grade "Twelfth grade") for public school attend the [North Warren Regional High School](/wiki/North_Warren_Regional_High_School "North Warren Regional High School") in Blairstown, a public secondary high school, serving students from the townships of Blairstown, [Frelinghuysen](/wiki/Frelinghuysen_Township,_New_Jersey "Frelinghuysen Township, New Jersey"), [Hardwick](/wiki/Hardwick_Township,_New_Jersey "Hardwick Township, New Jersey") and [Knowlton](/wiki/Knowlton_Township,_New_Jersey "Knowlton Township, New Jersey").[[84]](#cite_note-84)[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86) As of the 2021–22 school year, the high school had an enrollment of 658 students and 56.6 classroom teachers (on an [FTE](/wiki/Full-time_equivalent "Full-time equivalent") basis), for a [student–teacher ratio](/wiki/Student%E2%80%93teacher_ratio "Student–teacher ratio") of 11.6:1.[[87]](#cite_note-87)

Ridge and Valley Charter School, a K–8 [charter school](/wiki/Charter_school "Charter school") founded in 2004 that is focused on Earth literacy and sustainable living, is located in neighboring Frelinghuysen Township. The school also serves (and grants admission priority to) students from Frelinghuysen, Hardwick and Knowlton Townships, who attend the school without cost to the parents.[[88]](#cite_note-88) Students from the township and from all of Warren County are also eligible to attend [Warren County Technical School](/wiki/Warren_County_Technical_School "Warren County Technical School") in [Washington borough](/wiki/Washington,_New_Jersey "Washington, New Jersey") (for 9–12),[[89]](#cite_note-89) with special education services provided by local districts supplemented throughout the county by the [Warren County Special Services School District](/wiki/Warren_County_Special_Services_School_District "Warren County Special Services School District") in [Oxford Township](/wiki/Oxford_Township,_New_Jersey "Oxford Township, New Jersey") (for PreK–12).[[90]](#cite_note-90)[[80]](#cite_note-WarrenDistricts-80)

Students from across the world attend [Blair Academy](/wiki/Blair_Academy "Blair Academy"), a private boarding school for students in grades 9–12 established in 1848 by philanthropist [John Insley Blair](/wiki/John_Insley_Blair "John Insley Blair").[[91]](#cite_note-91)

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Blairstown,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Transportation")]

### Roads and highways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Blairstown,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Roads and highways")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/2021-06-16_10_21_30_View_west_along_the_westbound_lanes_of_Interstate_80_from_the_overpass_for_Union_Brick_Road_in_Blairstown_Township%2C_Warren_County%2C_New_Jersey.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:2021-06-16_10_21_30_View_west_along_the_westbound_lanes_of_Interstate_80_from_the_overpass_for_Union_Brick_Road_in_Blairstown_Township,_Warren_County,_New_Jersey.jpg)

[Interstate 80](/wiki/Interstate_80_in_New_Jersey "Interstate 80 in New Jersey") in Blairstown

As of May 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Blairstown,_New_Jersey&action=edit), the township had a total of 85.92 miles (138.27 km) of roadways, of which 61.05 miles (98.25 km) were maintained by the municipality, 17.23 miles (27.73 km) by Warren County and 7.64 miles (12.30 km) by the [New Jersey Department of Transportation](/wiki/New_Jersey_Department_of_Transportation "New Jersey Department of Transportation").[[92]](#cite_note-92)

[Interstate 80](/wiki/Interstate_80_in_New_Jersey "Interstate 80 in New Jersey") (the Bergen-Passaic Expressway) passes through the southern part of the township for 1.2 miles (1.9 km) without any interchanges, and is accessible via Route 94 and CR 521 in neighboring [Knowlton](/wiki/Knowlton_Township,_New_Jersey "Knowlton Township, New Jersey") and [Hope](/wiki/Hope_Township,_New_Jersey "Hope Township, New Jersey") townships, respectively.[[93]](#cite_note-93) [Route 94](/wiki/New_Jersey_Route_94 "New Jersey Route 94") passes for 6.5 miles (10.5 km) runs east–west through the center of the township while [County Route 521](/wiki/County_Route_521_(New_Jersey) "County Route 521 (New Jersey)") passes through in the eastern section.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

### Airport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Blairstown,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Airport")]

[Blairstown Airport](/wiki/Blairstown_Airport "Blairstown Airport") (1N7) is located southwest of the [central business district](/wiki/Central_business_district "Central business district").[[95]](#cite_note-95) The airport serves small planes and gliders.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

### Railroads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Blairstown,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Railroads")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Blairstown_Station.jpg/250px-Blairstown_Station.jpg)](/wiki/File:Blairstown_Station.jpg)

The former [New York, Susquehanna and Western Railroad](/wiki/New_York,_Susquehanna_and_Western_Railroad "New York, Susquehanna and Western Railroad") station site in Blairstown in October 2011

The [Lackawanna Cut-Off](/wiki/Lackawanna_Cut-Off "Lackawanna Cut-Off"), a high-speed, double-track railway line that stretches for 28.45 miles (45.79 km), was constructed by the [Delaware, Lackawanna and Western Railroad](/wiki/Delaware,_Lackawanna_and_Western_Railroad "Delaware, Lackawanna and Western Railroad") between 1908 and 1911, opening for service on December 24, 1911. It ran west from [Port Morris, New Jersey](/wiki/Port_Morris,_New_Jersey "Port Morris, New Jersey") to [Slateford, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Slateford,_Pennsylvania "Slateford, Pennsylvania") and passed through Blairstown. The DL&W RR merged with the [Erie Railroad](/wiki/Erie_Railroad "Erie Railroad") on October 17, 1960, to form the [Erie Lackawanna Railroad](/wiki/Erie_Lackawanna_Railroad "Erie Lackawanna Railroad"). Due to declining revenues, passenger service over the Lackawanna Cut-Off was discontinued on January 6, 1970, and freight service ceased in 1979, just three years after the E-L was absorbed into the Consolidated Railroad Corporation ([Conrail](/wiki/Conrail "Conrail")). The tracks remained relatively-dormant until 1984, when the property was sold to a developer and the rails were removed.

The right-of-way is now the property of the State of New Jersey, and plans are underway for the restoration of rail service in the future. As part of [restoring train service](/wiki/Lackawanna_Cut-Off_Restoration_Project "Lackawanna Cut-Off Restoration Project") by [New Jersey Transit](/wiki/New_Jersey_Transit "New Jersey Transit") via the [Lackawanna Cut-Off](/wiki/Lackawanna_Cut-Off "Lackawanna Cut-Off"), $61 million had been secured to restore railbed and tracks of the former railroad, with passenger service anticipated to be restored in 2026 or 2027.[[97]](#cite_note-opening-date-97) Blairstown's poured concrete [passenger and freight stations](/wiki/Blairstown_station "Blairstown station") still stand, although privately owned. The restored service plans include a stop at the Blairstown station as part of extension of Amtrak service to the [Poconos](/wiki/Pocono_Mountains "Pocono Mountains") and [Scranton, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Scranton,_Pennsylvania "Scranton, Pennsylvania").[[98]](#cite_note-98)

Blairstown was also served by a second railroad, the [Blairstown Railway](/wiki/Blairstown_Railway "Blairstown Railway"). The short line, a personal project of the local industrial magnate [John Insley Blair](/wiki/John_Insley_Blair "John Insley Blair"), was constructed in 1876 from Blairstown to [Delaware Township](/wiki/Delaware_Township,_Hunterdon_County,_New_Jersey "Delaware Township, Hunterdon County, New Jersey"), where it connected with the [Old Main Line](/wiki/Lackawanna_Old_Road "Lackawanna Old Road") of the Lackawanna RR. The Blairstown Railway was absorbed by the [New York, Susquehanna & Western Railroad](/wiki/New_York,_Susquehanna_%26_Western_Railroad "New York, Susquehanna & Western Railroad") in 1882 as it built west to the coal fields of Pennsylvania. The NYS&W also operated passenger service between Blairstown and New York (via Jersey City, NJ) until 1935. A third railroad, the [Lehigh and New England Railroad](/wiki/Lehigh_and_New_England_Railroad "Lehigh and New England Railroad"), operated through Blairstown via trackage rights over the NYS&W between Swartswood Junction and Hainesburg Junction until October 31, 1961, when the L&NE was abandoned. With the loss of L&NE trackage rights revenues and little local business to sustain the line, the NYS&W also abandoned its line through Blairstown shortly thereafter, and the tracks were removed in 1962. The right-of-way today has been preserved by the State of New Jersey as the 26-mile (42 km) long [Paulinskill Valley Trail](/wiki/Paulinskill_Valley_Trail "Paulinskill Valley Trail").

## Landmarks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Blairstown,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Landmarks")]

* [Blairstown Historic District](/wiki/Blairstown_Historic_District "Blairstown Historic District") was added to the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") in 2007.[[99]](#cite_note-nrhpdoc-99)
* Historic Blairstown Theater (also known as Roy's Hall) was built in 1913 as a silent movie house. The building was restored and painted blue in 2005 and is the centerpiece of Blairstown's vintage Main Street, surrounded by charming shops, galleries and restaurants. The HBT features a regular schedule of live music and theatrical performances, classic film and community events.[[100]](#cite_note-100)
* The Blairstown Museum is a private, non-profit corporation organized under New Jersey law managed by a board of directors and housed in a 19th-century building, known as the last remaining structure of "Roy's Row". The Museum is the only general history and cultural museum for the Township of Blairstown. It cares for over 2,000 items that illustrate the history of the township and its inhabitants, including former resident and namesake John Insley Blair.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

## Popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Blairstown,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Popular culture")]

* Scenes from the horror film *[Friday the 13th](/wiki/Friday_the_13th_(1980_film) "Friday the 13th (1980 film)")* were filmed on Blairstown's Main Street, and at the Blairstown Diner on Route 94; the Boy Scout camp [No-Be-Bo-Sco](/wiki/Northern_New_Jersey_Council#Camp_No-Be-Bo-Sco "Northern New Jersey Council") in adjacent [Hardwick Township](/wiki/Hardwick_Township,_New_Jersey "Hardwick Township, New Jersey") was the site for [Camp Crystal Lake](/wiki/Summer_camp "Summer camp").[[102]](#cite_note-102)
* The body of [Dawn Olanick](/wiki/Dawn_Olanick "Dawn Olanick"), formerly known as 'Princess Doe' was discovered at the Cedar Ridge Cemetery in Blairstown on July 15, 1982. She became the first unidentified body entered into the FBI's [NCIC](/wiki/NCIC "NCIC") computer system.[[103]](#cite_note-103)
* Scenes from the horror film *Plasterhead* were filmed at the Blairstown Diner on [Route 94](/wiki/New_Jersey_Route_94 "New Jersey Route 94") as part of an homage by the filmmakers to *Friday the 13th*.[[104]](#cite_note-104)
